title: Daily Briefing — Saturday, June 27
tldr: Web-design play of the day: stop re-explaining your design system to AI every time — package your team's rules into one Figma 'Skill' the design agent obeys on command. At Config 2026 (Jun 24 2026) Figma's on-canvas design agent went to open beta for everyone and gained custom Skills (plus MCP Connectors and file/PDF context). A Skill is a reusable instruction set you invoke as a slash command: name it follow-ds-guidelines and it becomes /follow-ds-guidelines. Write your conventions once in plain Markdown — which library/components to use, token names, the spacing scale and type ramp, do/don't rules, accessibility minimums — and every teammate's agent designs to your system instead of generic defaults. The #1 failure of AI-in-design is output that ignores your tokens and forces rework; a Skill is the governance lever that fixes it, no engineering required. Run-it-today: open the agent's prompt box → Skills → Add skill → Start from scratch, paste your DS rules as numbered steps + one worked example, then test with /follow-ds-guidelines design a settings page. (Open beta, free of AI credits during beta — credits apply at GA; needs a paid plan (Pro/Org/Enterprise) with AI on and a Full seat for Design/Make files; not on Starter/Education/Gov. New agent threads are visible to Full-seat teammates by default — set sensitive chats private. Distinct from Code Layers, which is closed beta starting July.)

Growth play: Google now shows you — first-party — how often your pages appear inside AI Overviews and AI Mode. Open the report and set your baseline today. Google Search Console has a Generative AI performance report (a dedicated view of your impressions in Google's AI features), and on Jun 25 2026 John Mueller publicly clarified exactly how those impressions are counted. Until now AI-search visibility could only be estimated by third-party tools (Semrush, Ahrefs) — this is the source of truth, straight from Google, segmentable by page/country/device. The play: open Performance → Generative AI, export your top and bottom URLs by AI-feature impressions, date-stamp it as your baseline, then double down on the pages already winning AI citations and rewrite the gaps to match. Read the numbers correctly per Mueller: an impression fires only when a real link to your page is shown (a favicon alone doesn't count); links that require a user to expand them count only once expanded; the same site shown twice in one AI answer is one impression. (Beta, rolling out to a subset of properties — you may not see it yet; impressions only right now, no clicks/CTR/queries. This resolves our Jun 21 'Google ships a first-party AI-visibility metric' call — ✅ with a caveat: the report had actually launched Jun 3, before we logged the call, and it's impressions-only, not a Bing-style citation-share metric.)

AI-tooling play: hand a fast, cheap model the boring browser work that has no API — and let it click through it for you, in a sandbox. On Jun 24 2026 Google moved 'computer use' into mainline Gemini 3.5 Flash (gemini-3.5-flash) as a built-in tool: one model that sees a screen and acts — clicks, types, scrolls, navigates — across browser, mobile and desktop, in public preview via the Gemini API. It scores 78.4 on OSWorld-Verified (a hair behind GPT-5.5's 78.7) at $1.50 in / $9 out per million tokens (~⅓ of GPT-5.5), so it's cheap enough to run daily. It kills GUI-bound grunt work with no export button: pulling numbers out of ad/analytics dashboards, filling repetitive intake/QA forms, scheduled competitor screenshots, smoke-testing your own app's UI before launch. Run-it-today: try the zero-code Browserbase demo linked from the docs, grab a key in Google AI Studio, then clone Google's Docker-sandboxed reference repo so the agent never touches your real machine. CRITICAL safety: agents that browse live pages are exposed to prompt injection (malicious instructions hidden in a page — attacks are already being reported) — before any real task, flip ON the two opt-in safeguards (confirm-before-sensitive-actions + auto-halt-on-injection, both OFF by default), and layer a sandbox + website allowlist + human-in-the-loop. Google says no single safeguard is enough.

Also moved — the AI landscape (cross-checked; markets closed, weekend): the frontier's best models are now shipping under explicit US-government gatekeeping. Anthropic's Claude Mythos 5 is back — the US government notified Anthropic it can be redeployed to a defined set of US critical-infrastructure operators (reported as 100+ companies and agencies; Commerce Secretary Lutnick: 'appropriate safeguards are in place'). This resolves our Jun 13 call ✅ (Anthropic restores access to at least one of the two suspended models) — but it's a restricted re-release: Fable 5 general access is still suspended, no timeline. Meanwhile OpenAI began a limited preview of GPT-5.6 in three variants (Sol flagship, Terra everyday, Luna fast/cheap) — and at the White House's request the government approves access customer-by-customer during preview (OpenAI: 'we don't believe this kind of government access process should become the long-term default'). And the AI-memory crunch is now hitting consumer hardware: Apple raised Mac and iPad prices on Jun 25, explicitly blaming surging memory/storage-chip costs from the AI data-center buildout ('we have never seen a component price increase this much, this quickly') — a demand-confirming signal for the same AI-memory trade (SK Hynix/Samsung/Micron). (Also: IBM debuted the first sub-1nm chip tech, ~5 years from production.)

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is state your rules once, then make the machine read its own reality. A Figma Skill states your design system once so every agent obeys it; Google's AI report makes you read your actual AI-search presence instead of guessing; Gemini's computer-use agent reads the live screen and acts on it — inside a sandbox you control. And the landscape just drew the boundary on all of it: the very best models now arrive gated and approved, while the AI-memory bill lands on a laptop near you. Plan client work around the tier you can actually get today, write your conventions down so the AI inherits them, and keep a human on the kill-switch.

sections.h: Web design — run it today: teach the Figma design agent your design system once, as a reusable Skill (slash command) every teammate's agent obeys

blocks.sub: Config 2026 (Jun 24 2026): Figma's on-canvas design agent went to open beta for everyone and gained custom Skills, MCP Connectors and file/PDF context — a Skill is a Markdown instruction set you invoke as a slash command (name it 'follow-ds-guidelines' → '/follow-ds-guidelines') so the agent designs to YOUR tokens/components/rules instead of generic defaults; you can also upload a .md skill (Agent Skills spec) or attach a brief and connect GitHub/Slack/Notion/Linear
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: at Config 2026 on Jun 24 2026 Figma launched its on-canvas design agent to everyone (open beta) and gave it three ways to take on your context — custom Skills, MCP Connectors, and file/PDF/spreadsheet attachments. The one to run today is Skills. A Skill is a reusable, shareable instruction set — written in plain Markdown — that you invoke as a slash command: the Skill's name becomes the command, so a Skill named follow-ds-guidelines is invoked as /follow-ds-guidelines. Inside it you encode your team's actual conventions: which component library to pull from, your token names, the spacing scale and type ramp, do/don't rules, accessibility minimums (contrast, hit-target size). Figma's how-to recommends an ordered Instructions section plus at least one concrete Examples block showing input → expected output. Because the Skill is just a file, you can also upload a .md written to the Agent Skills spec (the same format coding agents use) and reuse it across tools, and you can attach a brief or wire an MCP Connector (GitHub, Slack, Notion, Atlassian, Hex, Linear) to pull live tickets/data into the design and write updates back. Why it matters for us: the single most common failure of AI-in-design is output that ignores your design system — wrong components, off-scale spacing, invented tokens — which means rework that eats the time the AI was supposed to save. A Skill turns your design-system rules into a portable, version-controllable instruction the agent obeys on command, so 'AI that guesses' becomes 'AI that follows our system.' It's a governance lever for design systems, available now, no engineering required.
blocks.doIt: (1) Check access: you need a paid plan (Professional/Organization/Enterprise) with AI features enabled and a Full seat to use the agent in Design/Make files (View/Dev/Collab seats only get it in Draft files); it's not on Starter/Education/Government. (2) Create the Skill: open the agent's prompt box → Skills → Add skill → Start from scratch; fill Name (becomes your slash command), Description (when to use it), and Instructions (Markdown). (3) Encode your DS in the Instructions as numbered, imperative steps — the library/components to use, token names, spacing scale, type ramp, do/don't rules, a11y minimums — and add one Examples block (a request → the correct output). Click Add. (4) Test it: type /follow-ds-guidelines plus a request (e.g. 'design a settings page using our DS') and check the output respects your rules; iterate the Instructions text until it does. (5) Scale it: reuse an existing skill via Skills → Add skill → Upload a file (drag in a .md per the Agent Skills spec). (6) Add live context where useful: attach a brief/PDF to the thread, or add an MCP Connector to pull a ticket's spec into the design. (7) Privacy: as of Jun 23 2026 new agent threads are visible by default to Full-seat teammates with edit access — set sensitive chats private. Card: card-webdesign-figma-agent-skills.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Reported (2) — Figma's own primary posts (the 'agent: custom tools, context, skills' blog, the Config 2026 recap, and the Help Center 'Custom skills for the Figma agent and Figma Make' how-to — all figma.com) plus independent press (Web Developer's Config 2026 write-up, which describes the agent's Skills 'like agent skills in coding tools' + MCP connectors). Honest scope: open beta as of Jun 24 2026, rolling out gradually and free of AI credits during beta — credits WILL apply at GA (and prompting the agent to build plugins/shaders already costs credits), so cost it before standardizing a batch workflow; gated to paid plans + Full seats for Design/Make (Draft-only on View/Dev/Collab; excluded: Starter/Education/Gov); custom Skills are instructions-only (no scripts//references//assets/ dirs). Distinct from Code Layers ('code on the canvas'), which is closed beta, early access starting July via figma.com/config-betas — NOT run-it-today — and from Figma Motion (card-webdesign-figma-motion) and Shader fills/effects (card-graphic-figma-shaders), the other Config 2026 drops. Joins the design-tooling/handoff branch. Logged a prediction (Skills become billable AI-credit actions at GA AND/OR a community Skills marketplace + a cross-tool .md skill standard emerge). Sources: Figma Blog (agent skills), Figma Learn (Custom skills how-to), Figma Blog (Config 2026 recap), Web Developer.

sections.h: Growth — run it today: open Google's first-party AI-visibility report in Search Console, baseline your AI Overviews / AI Mode impressions, and read them correctly per Mueller's counting rules

blocks.sub: Google Search Console now has a Generative AI performance report — your impressions inside AI Overviews, AI Mode and AI features in Discover, segmentable by page/country/device — and on Jun 25 2026 John Mueller clarified how impressions are counted (a link to your page must actually be shown; a favicon doesn't count; expandable links count only once expanded; the same site twice in one answer = one impression). It's the first-party source of truth that replaces third-party AEO estimators
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: Google Search Console exposes a Generative AI performance report — a dedicated view of how often your URLs appear inside Google's AI Overviews, AI Mode, and AI features in Discover — and on Jun 25 2026 Google's John Mueller publicly clarified the rules for how those impressions are counted. This is significant because, until now, AI-search visibility could only be inferred by third-party estimators (Semrush's free checker, Ahrefs Brand Radar, Bing's AI Citation Share — all of which we've covered). This is the first-party source of truth from Google, segmentable by page, country, device and date. The counting rules matter for reading it honestly (Mueller, Jun 25): an impression is logged only when an actual link to your page is shown in the AI feature — a favicon or icon with no link does not count; a link that requires the user to activate/expand it counts only once expanded (so the reported number can look lower than your true presence); and if the same site appears twice in one AI answer, that's one impression. Separately, in the classic Performance report the same URL appearing in both an AI Overview and the blue links is counted as one impression at the AIO's position — so don't double-count across reports. Why it matters for us: buyers increasingly start at an AI answer, and we've been optimizing AEO against estimates. Getting an early, dated baseline from Google's own data — while it's still in beta and competitors aren't watching — lets us correlate specific content changes to actual AI-feature visibility, instead of guessing.
blocks.doIt: (1) Open the report: in Google Search Console pick your property → Performance in the left nav; if you're in the rollout, a Generative AI report sits alongside Search results and Discover. (2) Don't see it? It's gated to a subset of properties — verify the property (domain-level helps) and check the Discover variant too; it may simply not be enabled for you yet. (3) Read the Pages breakdown: pull your top-3 and bottom-3 URLs by AI-feature impressions — your 'already cited by Google AI' winners and your gaps. (4) Segment by Country and Device to see where your AI visibility concentrates; export to a sheet and date-stamp it as your baseline. (5) Act on it: map the high-impression URLs against the AEO formats that win citations (intent-matched title, one-sentence answer up top, list/table, an original first-party stat, FAQ schema + last-updated date — see card-growth-aeo-content-formats and card-growth-schema-aeo); double down on those patterns and rewrite low-impression pages to match. (6) Track over time: there's no API field yet, so set a recurring weekly export so you can prove cause-and-effect after you ship changes. (7) Interpret with the counting rules above so you don't over- or under-read a number. Card: card-growth-ai-visibility-check (updated).
blocks.note: Corroboration: Reported (2) — Google's own primary docs (Search Console Help 'Generative AI performance report' for Search and for Discover, plus the Search Central blog announcement — all google.com) + Search Engine Journal (Mueller's impression-counting explanation, Jun 25, independent). Honest scope: beta, rolling out to a subset of website owners — you may not have it yet, and early access has looked uneven by region; it is impressions only right now (no clicks, CTR, average position, or query data in this report — verified in Google's Help doc; one auto-summary that claimed clicks/CTR was a misread); and it groups AI Overviews + AI Mode under 'generative AI features,' so you can't cleanly isolate one from the other. Resolves the open Jun 21 prediction ('Google ships a first-party AI-citation/answer-visibility metric in Search Console') — ✅ on the substance, with an honest caveat: the report had already launched Jun 3 2026 (before we logged the Jun 21 call — we missed it; the Jun 25 Mueller clarification is what resurfaced it), and it's impressions-only, not the competitor citation-share metric the call benchmarked against Bing. Advances the answer-engine-optimization thread (first-party measurement leg). Logged a new prediction (Google adds clicks and/or CTR — possibly query data — to the Generative AI performance report before GA). Sources: Google Search Console Help (Search), Google Search Console Help (Discover), Google Search Central Blog, Search Engine Journal.

sections.h: AI tooling — run it today: point Gemini 3.5 Flash 'computer use' at your repetitive browser ops and let it click through them, inside a Docker sandbox you control

blocks.sub: Jun 24 2026: Google moved 'computer use' into mainline Gemini 3.5 Flash (gemini-3.5-flash) as a built-in tool — one fast/cheap model that sees a screen and acts (click, type, scroll, navigate, drag) across browser/mobile/desktop, in public preview via the Gemini API; 78.4 on OSWorld-Verified (vs GPT-5.5's 78.7) at $1.50 in / $9 out per M tokens (~⅓ of GPT-5.5), with a zero-code Browserbase demo and an official Docker-sandboxed reference repo
blocks.p: What changed / the technique: on Jun 24 2026 Google moved its 'computer use' capability from a standalone model into mainline Gemini 3.5 Flash (gemini-3.5-flash) as a native, built-in tool. One fast, cheap model can now see a screen, reason, and act — clicking, typing, scrolling, navigating and dragging — across browser, mobile and desktop environments, available in public preview today via the Gemini API (and the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, the renamed Vertex AI). It scores 78.4 on OSWorld-Verified (the standard computer-use benchmark) — within a fraction of GPT-5.5's 78.7 — at $1.50 in / $9 out per million tokens (~one-third of GPT-5.5's $5/$30), so it's cheap and fast enough to run on a schedule. The action loop is simple: the model returns a function call (an action + its reasoning + normalized 0–999 coordinates); your client scales the coordinates to the viewport, executes the action (Google's reference uses Playwright), captures a fresh screenshot, and sends it back — repeating until the task is done. Why it matters for us: this kills the long tail of GUI-bound grunt work that has no API and no export button — pulling numbers out of ad/analytics dashboards that won't export, filling repetitive intake/QA forms, scheduled competitor screenshots, bulk updates in a content portal, and smoke-testing your own web app's UI flows before a launch. At Flash price/speed those become a daily loop, not a manual chore.
blocks.doIt: (1) Feel the loop with zero code: open the Browserbase-hosted demo linked from the official docs and give it a plain-English task ('open this dashboard, read the 7-day spend, screenshot it'). (2) Get an API key in Google AI Studio (ai.google.dev), prototype a prompt, then hit 'Get code' to export the Gemini API call in your language. (3) Clone the official reference repo (google-gemini/computer-use-preview) — it ships a ready-to-use Docker sandbox so the agent never touches your real machine. (4) Set the model to gemini-3.5-flash with the computer-use tool enabled (you can co-activate Search, code execution and function calling on the same agent). (5) Flip ON the two opt-in safeguards before any real task — they're OFF by default: (a) require explicit user confirmation on sensitive/irreversible actions, (b) auto-terminate the task on detected prompt injection. (6) Lock the blast radius: run only in the Docker/VM sandbox, apply a website allowlist, keep a human in the loop for purchases/sends, and turn on audit logging. (7) Start low-stakes: point it at an internal task first (e.g. a UI regression pass on your staging site) before anything that spends money or sends messages. Card: card-ai-tooling-gemini-computer-use.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Confirmed (3) — Google primary (the blog.google announcement + the ai.google.dev Gemini API 'computer use' docs; note the DeepMind blog URL 302-redirects to the same Google post, so it's not an independent source) + The Next Web + Search Engine Journal (the security angle). Honest scope: public preview — Google's own docs warn it 'may contain security vulnerabilities' and to supervise closely; access is via the Gemini API / Enterprise Agent Platform, pay-as-you-go, no special gating beyond a key (genuinely run-it-today, unlike GPT-5.6 'Sol' which stays US-gov / customer-by-customer gated — see today's news). The big caveat is indirect prompt injection: agents that browse live pages can be hijacked by malicious instructions hidden in a page or document, and press is already reporting real attacks (e.g. a poisoned skills file driving unauthorized purchases) — Google explicitly says 'no individual safeguard is sufficient,' so layer sandbox + allowlist + human confirmation + injection auto-halt + logging. Prices/benchmarks directional. Joins the agent-autonomy/safety-scaffold thread (related: card-ai-tooling-claude-loop-goal, card-ai-tooling-model-portability). Logged a prediction (a 2nd frontier lab ships a fast-tier computer-use/browser-control model at ≤~$2/$10 per M tokens to match Gemini 3.5 Flash). Sources: Google (blog.google), Google AI for Developers (Gemini API docs), The Next Web, Search Engine Journal.

sections.h: News — the AI landscape (cross-checked; US markets closed for the weekend, so this is the what-companies-are-building half)

blocks.sub: The frontier's best models now ship under explicit US-government gatekeeping: Anthropic's Claude Mythos 5 is back but RESTRICTED to ~100+ US critical-infrastructure operators (Fable 5 general access still suspended, no timeline) — resolving our Jun 13 restoration call ✅ — while OpenAI's GPT-5.6 (Sol/Terra/Luna) ships via a customer-by-customer government-approved preview; separately Apple raised Mac/iPad prices Jun 25, blaming AI-driven memory-chip cost spikes (a demand-confirming signal for the SK Hynix/Samsung/Micron memory trade)
blocks.p: What moved (all cross-checked; weekend, no live prices): the throughline this week is frontier-model availability becoming a government decision. (1) Anthropic's Claude Mythos 5 is restored — but restricted. The US government notified Anthropic that Mythos 5 (its cybersecurity model, dark since the Jun 12 export-control directive) can be redeployed to a defined set of US critical-infrastructure operators — reported as 100+ companies and agencies — under agreed safeguards (Commerce Secretary Lutnick: 'appropriate safeguards are in place'). Anthropic's statement: it has been 'working closely with the US government to restore access' and is still working toward the return of Claude Fable 5 for unrestricted use — which remains suspended with no timeline. This resolves our Jun 13 prediction ✅ ('Anthropic restores access to at least one of the two models') — honestly, a partial/restricted restoration, not a full one. (Confirmed ≥3: Engadget, NBC News, CNN headlines + Anthropic's own statement via Cybersecurity News; the exact recipient count varies by outlet — treat '~100+' as directional, and don't conflate it with a separate Jun 2 Glasswing-expansion '150 orgs/15 countries' figure.) (2) OpenAI's GPT-5.6 ships gated. OpenAI began a limited preview of GPT-5.6 in three variants — Sol (flagship/strongest), Terra (balanced everyday), Luna (fast/cheap) — to a small group of trusted partners, and at the White House's request the government approves access customer-by-customer during preview (under the Office of the National Cyber Director + OSTP), with broad release 'in the coming weeks.' OpenAI: 'we don't believe this kind of government access process should become the long-term default.' (Confirmed ≥3: OpenAI's own wording via Simon Willison, Engadget, The Decoder; the specific partner count (~20) is single-source/directional. Variant pricing per OpenAI, directional: Sol $5/$30, Terra $2.50/$15, Luna $1/$6 per M tokens.) (3) The AI-memory crunch reaches consumer hardware. Apple raised prices on Macs and iPads on Jun 25 (iPhone/Watch/AirPods spared), explicitly blaming surging memory/storage-chip costs as makers prioritize AI data-center orders — 'we have never seen a component price increase this much, this quickly' (e.g. MacBook Air 512GB $1,099→$1,299; iPad Air 128GB $599→$749). It's the same shortage lifting SK Hynix/Samsung/Micron — a demand-confirming, not demand-collapsing, signal for the AI-memory trade. (Confirmed ≥3: Fox Business, MacRumors + Reuters/Bloomberg headlines.) (4) Quick hit: IBM debuted the first sub-1nm (0.7nm/7-angstrom) chip tech using 3D 'nanostack' transistors (~100B transistors on a fingernail; up to 50% more performance or 70% better efficiency vs 2nm) — but it's ~5 years from production, so a roadmap signal, not a near-term lever (Confirmed ≥3: IBM Newsroom, HowToGeek + MIT Tech Review/Ars headlines). Why it matters for us — the climate signal: the 'newest model, instantly available to everyone' era is over for the frontier tier. (a) The single best model may not be in your account on day one — scope client work around the generally-available tier (Terra/Luna-, Sonnet-class), not an embargoed flagship. (b) 'Which model, what governance, is it cleared?' is becoming a procurement question, not just a quality one — AI provenance is moving up the buying checklist. (c) Budget for drift: Apple's hikes show AI-memory demand now bleeds into hardware costs, so compute/device line items will creep up. Thread impact: the frontier-model-governance thread advances from 'switched off overnight' (Jun 12–13) to 'switched back on, but only for the cleared' — and the craft payoff is the model-portability scaffold (card-ai-tooling-model-portability). On the open V-shape 'dip not regime change' call [news, due ~Jul 20]: markets were closed; nothing material broke this weekend, and Apple's memory-cost hike reads as demand-confirming for the memory basket — call stays open, leaning held. Logged two new predictions (Fable 5 unrestricted access restored by ~Sep 30; GPT-5.6 reaches GA without per-customer approval by ~Aug 15). Sources: Engadget (Mythos), Cybersecurity News (Anthropic statement), Simon Willison (OpenAI GPT-5.6), Engadget (GPT-5.6 variants), The Decoder (customer-by-customer), Fox Business + MacRumors (Apple), IBM Newsroom + HowToGeek (IBM).

sources: Figma Blog — The Figma agent: custom tools, context, and skills · Figma Learn — Custom skills for the Figma agent and Figma Make · Figma Blog — Config 2026 recap · Web Developer — Figma Config 2026: Motion + design agent · Google Search Console Help — Generative AI performance report (Search) · Google Search Console Help — Generative AI performance report (Discover) · Search Engine Journal — Mueller explains how AI search impressions get counted · Google — Introducing computer use in Gemini 3.5 Flash · Google AI for Developers — Computer use (Gemini API docs) · The Next Web — Gemini 3.5 Flash computer use as a built-in tool · Search Engine Journal — Gemini can now control your computer; hackers are targeting AI agents · Engadget — US lets Anthropic redeploy Mythos cybersecurity AI · Cybersecurity News — Anthropic statement on Mythos 5 restoration · Simon Willison — Quoting OpenAI on the GPT-5.6 preview · Engadget — OpenAI previews GPT-5.6 and its three variants · The Decoder — GPT-5.6 rollout requires customer-by-customer US approval · Fox Business — Apple raises iPad/MacBook prices as memory costs surge · MacRumors — Apple raises Mac and iPad prices (memory/storage costs) · IBM Newsroom — World's first sub-1nm chip technology
